# **Thermodynamic Characterization and Heat Capacity Profiling of Ammonium Carbamate Solutions in Low-Pressure Urea Recovery Sections**

The industrial synthesis of urea via the Bazarov reaction relies fundamentally on the precise management of complex, highly non-ideal thermodynamic systems comprising ammonia, carbon dioxide, water, and urea1. While the high-pressure (HP) synthesis loop dictates the overall conversion efficiency and baseline energy consumption, the economic viability, operational stability, and environmental compliance of modern urea plants depend strictly on the performance of the downstream recovery sections. The Low-Pressure (LP) recovery section, typically operating between 3 to 5 bar and 60 to 120 °C, is tasked with condensing unreacted ammonia and carbon dioxide into an aqueous ammonium carbamate solution for subsequent recirculation2.  
The accurate determination of the isobaric heat capacity (![][image1]) of this multi-component aqueous carbamate solution is a paramount parameter for chemical and process engineers. It directly dictates the thermal sizing of LP carbamate condensers, the calculation of cooling water utility loads, and the highly sensitive thermal integration required to prevent the premature crystallization of solid carbamate or the excessive vaporization of free ammonia3. An underestimation of specific heat capacity can lead to undersized heat exchangers, resulting in inadequate heat removal, elevated vapor pressures, and an unacceptable slip of gaseous ammonia to the atmospheric vent scrubbers. Conversely, overestimating this property leads to unnecessary capital expenditure (CAPEX) due to over-designed condenser surface areas.  
This exhaustive report provides a highly granular thermodynamic analysis of the specific heat capacity of ammonium carbamate solutions under LP conditions. It evaluates pure component empirical equations, strictly sourced polynomial models, rigorous activity coefficient models (such as the Electrolyte Non-Random Two-Liquid (e-NRTL) model and the Extended UNIQUAC framework), and the intricate physical chemistry of reactive "apparent" heat capacities.

## **Theoretical Foundations of the Urea Synthesis Cycle**

Commercial urea production is achieved through the direct dehydration of ammonium carbamate at elevated temperatures (170–200 °C) and pressures (130–250 bar)3. The process is defined by two primary, simultaneous reactions. The first is the highly exothermic formation of ammonium carbamate from gaseous carbon dioxide and liquid ammonia:  
![][image2]  
Historical determinations of the heat of formation for ammonium carbamate from pure ammonia and carbon dioxide have varied widely. Early calorimetric studies utilizing direct methods in closed-bomb calorimeters, such as those conducted by Clark in 1927, measured the heat of formation at constant volume to be approximately 36,300 to 37,700 cal/mol, though theoretical calculations from van't Hoff equations have yielded values up to 46,090 cal/mol depending on the temperature range and phase states involved5.  
The second reaction is the slow, slightly endothermic dehydration of liquid ammonium carbamate to yield urea and water:  
![][image3]  
Because the dehydration reaction is equilibrium-limited—typically achieving 50–68% conversion depending on the initial ![][image4] molar ratio (N/C ratio) and ![][image5] molar ratio (H/C ratio)—the reactor effluent contains a substantial mixture of unconverted ammonium carbamate, free ammonia, and carbon dioxide3. The fundamental strategy of all modern urea technologies—whether the Stamicarbon ![][image6]\-stripping process, the Snamprogetti ![][image7]\-stripping process, or the Toyo ACES21 framework—is to decompose this unreacted carbamate in successive stages of progressively decreasing pressure7.  
The stripped gases (![][image7] and ![][image6]) generated in the high, medium, and low-pressure decomposers are subsequently recombined in respective carbamate condensers. Crucially, condensation must occur in the presence of water to prevent the deposition of solid ammonium carbamate crystals. Pure solid ammonium carbamate exhibits a high vapor pressure and sublimes at approximately 60 °C at atmospheric pressure, and melts near 150 °C under higher pressures with a heat of fusion of approximately 16.74 kJ/mol3. The resulting fluid, recycled sequentially back to the high-pressure synthesis loop, is colloquially termed an "ammonium carbamate solution," though thermodynamically, it is a highly concentrated, dense aqueous mixture of ionic and molecular species.

## **The Low-Pressure (LP) Recovery Section Environment**

Within the LP section, the system pressure is reduced to approximately 3.5 to 5 bar absolute1. The liquid effluent arriving from the Medium-Pressure (MP) section (or directly from the HP stripper in older conventional once-through plants) is subjected to further heating via low-pressure steam to strip the remaining volatile reactants2. The overhead vapors from this LP decomposer, which are heavily laden with ![][image7], ![][image6], and water vapor, are routed directly to the LP carbamate condenser.  
At these highly specific LP conditions, the condensation temperature is acutely sensitive to the partial pressures of the gas components, the molar ratios, and the available cooling utility (typically cooling water or a process integration stream). The condensation of excess ammonia, for example, is governed by a strict thermodynamic relationship between the condensation temperature and the latent heat of condensation. As the condensation temperature approaches the critical temperature of ammonia, the heat of condensation diminishes toward zero11.  
Simultaneously, the specific heat of the absorbing liquid medium is required to absorb both the sensible heat of the incoming hot gases and the immense heat of solution and reaction as carbamate bonds are formed. If the specific heat capacity of the circulating aqueous carbamate solution is mischaracterized, the resulting thermal duty profile of the condenser will be inherently flawed.

## **Speciation and Physical Chemistry of Aqueous Ammonium Carbamate**

To accurately profile the heat capacity of an ammonium carbamate solution, it is imperative to deconstruct the term "solution" at a molecular level. When ammonium carbamate is synthesized or dissolved in water, it undergoes immediate partial hydrolysis and ionic dissociation. The liquid phase is not a simple binary or ternary mixture of molecular ![][image8], free ![][image7], and ![][image9]. Instead, it consists of a highly interdependent network of chemical equilibria12:

> 1. Dissociation of the water solvent: ![][image10]  
> 2. Protonation of free ammonia: ![][image11]  
> 3. Hydration of carbon dioxide: ![][image12]  
> 4. Dissociation of bicarbonate: ![][image13]  
> 5. Formation of the carbamate ion: ![][image14]  
> 6. Solid precipitation limits (if saturated): ![][image15] and ![][image16]

At the elevated temperatures typical of the LP section (60–100 °C), the thermodynamic equilibrium shifts heavily toward the formation of the carbamate ion (![][image17]) and the retention of free ![][image7] (aq), while bicarbonate (![][image18]) formation is less favored compared to the heavily chilled conditions used in specialized carbon capture processes (such as the Chilled Ammonia Process, which operates near 2–10 °C)13.  
Consequently, the true isobaric specific heat of this liquid solution (![][image19]) is the mole-fraction weighted sum of the partial molar heat capacities of all these individual ionic and molecular species, further adjusted by an excess heat capacity term that accounts for non-ideal solute-solute and solute-solvent interactions.

## **Baseline Heat Capacity Data for Pure Components**

Before defining the rigorous thermodynamic mixture models, it is necessary to establish the empirical heat capacity data for the pure macroscopic components that form the boundary conditions of the solution. The heat capacities of pure solid ammonium carbamate, liquid water, and liquid ammonia define the thermal extremes of the LP system.

### **Pure Solid and Molten Ammonium Carbamate**

Pure solid ammonium carbamate (![][image8]) exists as a colorless, rhombic crystalline powder at standard conditions10. Empirical measurements of the constant-pressure specific heat (![][image1]) for solid ammonium carbamate exhibit a profound positive temperature dependence. Historical and referenced calorimetric data provide discrete values spanning from room temperature up to the theoretical decomposition/melting thresholds3.

| Temperature (°C) | Temperature (K) | Specific Heat, Cp​ (J/g·K) | Specific Heat, Cp​ (kJ/kmol·K) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 20 | 293.15 | 1.67 | 130.38 |
| 60 | 333.15 | 1.92 | 149.89 |
| 100 | 373.15 | 2.18 | 170.19 |
| 140 | 413.15 | 2.43 | 189.71 |
| 180 | 453.15 | 2.59 | 202.20 |

As demonstrated in the table above, the specific heat of the carbamate species rises dramatically as thermal energy is applied. This steep gradient reflects the increasing vibrational and rotational degrees of freedom within the crystal lattice prior to the phase transition.

### **The Specific Heat of Liquid Ammonia and Water**

The specific heat of the principal solvent mediums—liquid ammonia and liquid water—provides the bulk baseline for the LP solution's thermal mass. The specific heat of liquid water is remarkably stable across the LP section temperature range, varying only slightly around 4.18 to 4.22 kJ/(kg·K)15.  
Liquid ammonia, however, exhibits a highly variable specific heat that rises aggressively with temperature as it approaches its critical point (132.4 °C). The specific heat of liquid ammonia is foundational to establishing the heat balance of any carbamate condenser, particularly in processes that operate with a vast stoichiometric excess of ammonia. Referenced historical data outlines this trajectory clearly11:

| Temperature (°C) | Specific Heat (kcal/kg·°C) | Specific Heat (kJ/kg·K) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 20 | 1.13 | 4.728 |
| 30 | 1.14 | 4.770 |
| 40 | 1.16 | 4.853 |
| 50 | 1.18 | 4.937 |
| 60 | 1.21 | 5.063 |
| 70 | 1.25 | 5.230 |
| 80 | 1.29 | 5.397 |
| 90 | 1.36 | 5.690 |
| 100 | 1.48 | 6.192 |
| 110 | 1.61 | 6.736 |

The exceptionally high specific heat of liquid ammonia at elevated temperatures (exceeding 6.0 kJ/kg·K above 100 °C) indicates that in LP carbamate solutions featuring a high N/C ratio, the bulk heat capacity of the mixture will be skewed heavily upward by the free ammonia content. This property requires LP condenser designs to account for substantial sensible heat loads when subcooling ammonia-rich mixtures.

### **Pure Urea and Aqueous Urea Solutions**

While the LP recovery section is primarily concerned with the ammonium carbamate equilibrium, trace amounts of urea are often present due to entrainment or specific process routing (such as in certain ACES21 or total recycle loops). Pure solid urea has a melting point of 132.7 °C to 135 °C and a specific heat that scales with temperature: 1.398 kJ/(kg·K) at 0 °C, 1.661 kJ/(kg·K) at 50 °C, and 1.887 kJ/(kg·K) at 100 °C3. Furthermore, the heat of crystallization of a 70% aqueous urea solution is cited as 460 J/g, representing a significant latent heat factor if process conditions deviate into solidification regimes3.

## **The Chauhan Empirical Equation for Ammonium Carbamate**

In proprietary process simulation software and generalized plant design heuristics (such as the parameters utilized in specific engineering audits and plant models), the discrete specific heat data for pure components is traditionally regressed into continuous polynomial equations. This mathematical continuity allows process simulators to seamlessly integrate the specific heat over a temperature differential (![][image20]) to calculate the specific enthalpy (![][image21]) of the streams entering and leaving the heat exchangers18.  
A rigorously sourced empirical equation for the specific heat of ammonium carbamate (treated as a continuous pseudo-liquid phase beyond its standard melting point, representing the pure molten salt) utilizes a standard polynomial format:  
![][image22]  
For ammonium carbamate, the evaluated empirical constants yield the following specific heat equation, where ![][image1] is expressed in kJ/(kg·K) and absolute temperature (![][image23]) is measured in Kelvin18:

* ![][image24]  
* ![][image25]  
* ![][image26]  
* ![][image27]

![][image28]  
Applying this empirical equation at the upper boundary of typical LP conditions, such as 90 °C (363.15 K), yields an apparent specific heat of approximately 2.08 kJ/(kg·K). At the extreme synthesis loop conditions of 180 °C (453.15 K), the equation yields 2.55 kJ/(kg·K), which tightly correlates with the experimentally determined value of 2.59 J/(g·K) from independent calorimetric evaluations3.  
The utility of this polynomial lies in its integration for precise sensible heat duties. For instance, to calculate the heat released from a pure carbamate stream cooling from 190 °C (463.15 K) down to 178 °C (451.15 K) inside a decomposer or condenser, the integral is applied directly:  
![][image29]  
While this equation is highly functional for macroscopic mass and energy balances assuming a non-reactive pseudo-fluid, it represents a "frozen" chemical state. Real LP carbamate solutions require more rigorous thermodynamic treatment to account for the ionic nature of the solvent-solute interactions.

## **Rigorous Thermodynamic Modeling of Solution Heat Capacity**

Modern chemical engineering requires thermodynamic rigor that accounts for the non-ideal mixing, the highly polar nature of the components, and the resulting ionic speciation occurring within the LP condenser. The interaction between highly concentrated ions and a highly polar solvent matrix drastically alters the thermal properties of the bulk fluid. Two premier activity coefficient models are deployed globally in the industry for the ![][image7]\-![][image6]\-![][image9] system: the Electrolyte Non-Random Two-Liquid (e-NRTL) model and the Extended UNIQUAC model19.

### **The Electrolyte NRTL (e-NRTL) Framework**

The e-NRTL model explicitly accounts for the intricate solution chemistry, incorporating the dissociations of water, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, alongside the formation of the carbamate anion (![][image17]) and the bicarbonate anion (![][image18])13. In this advanced framework, the heat capacity of the aqueous carbamate solution is not calculated via a single lumped polynomial (such as the Chauhan equation). Instead, the molar isobaric heat capacity of the liquid mixture (![][image19]) is strictly defined by the mole-fraction weighted sum of the partial molar heat capacities of the true underlying species, adjusted for the excess heat capacity of mixing.  
For molecular solutes and the solvent (water, molecular ![][image7], and molecular ![][image6]), the reference state is the ideal gas heat capacity (![][image30]) corrected for the enthalpy of vaporization and the infinite dilution state. For ionic species, however, the reference state is defined as the aqueous phase infinite dilution heat capacity (![][image31]). Due to the lack of high-temperature ionic heat capacity data and the relative stability of these values over the narrow LP range (60–120 °C), these infinite dilution heat capacities are generally modeled as temperature-independent constants13.  
The rigorously regressed infinite dilution heat capacities for the constituent ions present in the carbamate solution at 298.15 K are13:

* Hydronium ion (![][image32]): 75.291 J/(mol·K)  
* Hydroxide ion (![][image33]): \-148.45 J/(mol·K)  
* Ammonium ion (![][image34]): 79.962 J/(mol·K)  
* Bicarbonate ion (![][image18]): \-29.260 J/(mol·K)  
* Carbonate ion (![][image35]): \-397.10 J/(mol·K)  
* Carbamate ion (![][image17]): \-30.710 J/(mol·K)

The profoundly negative values assigned to the carbamate, bicarbonate, and carbonate ions are not mathematical anomalies; they reflect a fundamental physical chemistry phenomenon known as **electrostriction**. When these dense, highly charged polyatomic anions exist in an aqueous matrix, they exert a massive electrostatic pull on the surrounding water molecules. This forces the highly polar water molecules to arrange themselves into a rigid, tightly packed solvation shell. This rigid structural alignment significantly restricts the rotational, vibrational, and translational degrees of freedom of the local water solvent13. Because heat capacity is a macroscopic measure of a system's ability to store thermal energy within these molecular degrees of freedom, the restriction imposed by the ions effectively lowers the apparent heat capacity of the bulk solution relative to what one would expect from pure water.  
The final heat capacity of the mixture in the e-NRTL model is calculated by taking the exact speciation mole fractions (![][image36]), multiplying them by their respective partial molar heat capacities, and adding the excess heat capacity (![][image37]). The excess heat capacity is derived from the second derivative of the excess Gibbs free energy (![][image38]) with respect to temperature:  
![][image39]  
![][image40]  
By utilizing the binary interaction parameters (![][image41]) fitted against experimental vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) and calorimetric data, the e-NRTL model reliably predicts the thermal behavior of the fluid in the LP condenser, mitigating the risks of heat exchanger under-design21.

### **The Extended UNIQUAC Framework**

An alternative, equally robust formulation is the Extended UNIQUAC model, originally pioneered by Thomsen and Rasmussen and later specifically adapted by Darde, Maribo-Mogensen, and Voskov for urea synthesis and carbon capture conditions1. In the Voskov adaptation for the ![][image7]\-![][image6]\-![][image9]\-Urea system, the liquid phase constituents are modeled using fundamental structural volume (![][image42]) and surface area (![][image43]) parameters1.  
For ammonium carbamate treated as a discrete solvated constituent within the UNIQUAC matrix, the structural parameters are rigorously defined as ![][image44] and ![][image45]1.  
The Extended UNIQUAC approach excels in calculating the solid-liquid-vapor equilibria (SLVE) at LP conditions. This is critical because the LP carbamate solution must operate with a sufficient water margin to prevent solid carbamate from precipitating inside the condenser tubes—a phenomenon that rapidly scales the heat exchanger and forces a plant shutdown. The specific heat capacity derived from the UNIQUAC model matches empirical calorimetric data within a 5% average absolute deviation (AAD) across the 60–120 °C envelope, providing superior performance in resolving the highly non-ideal interactions between the urea matrix and the carbamate ions19.  
Furthermore, the vapor phase in these rigorous models must be described accurately, as the enthalpy of condensation heavily influences the thermal profile. The vapor phase is commonly modeled using the Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) equation of state or advanced Virial equations. For instance, the Virial equation of state by Voronin for the ternary gas mixture relies on temperature-dependent second and third virial coefficients (![][image46] and ![][image47]) to accurately predict the non-ideal compressibility of the highly polar ![][image7] and ![][image9] vapors1.

## **The "Apparent" Heat Capacity Anomaly: Reactive Thermodynamics**

A profound second-order insight arises when attempting to measure, model, or utilize the specific heat of an ammonium carbamate solution in a highly dynamic process environment, such as the falling-film tubes of a LP decomposer or the flooded bundles of an LP condenser.  
When thermal energy (sensible heat) is added to or removed from an ammonium carbamate solution, the applied heat does not exclusively alter the kinetic temperature of the molecules. Simultaneously, the changing temperature induces a rapid shift in the chemical equilibrium of the highly reversible carbamate formation reaction. Consequently, process engineers must critically distinguish between the **frozen** heat capacity and the **reactive** (or apparent) heat capacity.

> 1. **Frozen Heat Capacity (![][image48])**: The theoretical specific heat of the mixture assuming absolutely no chemical reactions or speciation shifts occur upon heating or cooling. This represents the simple mass-weighted summation of the species' inherent heat capacities.  
> 2. **Apparent Heat Capacity (![][image49])**: The actual specific heat observed in macroscopic reality, where a temperature change triggers the endothermic dissociation (upon heating) or the exothermic formation (upon cooling) of the carbamate ion network.

Mathematically, the apparent isobaric molar heat capacity is expressed as the sum of the frozen capacity and a reactive term:  
![][image50]  
Where ![][image51] is the molar enthalpy of the ![][image52]\-th reaction (e.g., the formation of ![][image17]), and ![][image53] is the partial derivative of the reaction extent (![][image54]) with respect to temperature at constant pressure.  
Because the heat of absorption and subsequent reaction of ![][image6] into aqueous ammonia to form carbamate is immensely exothermic—approximately 70 to 85 kJ/mol of ![][image6] absorbed, varying slightly depending on the exact ionic loading—the reactive term frequently dominates the thermal behavior of the LP section14.  
If a process engineer relies purely on a static polynomial equation (like the Chauhan equation yielding ![][image55] kJ/kg·K) for a dynamic LP decomposer, they will drastically underestimate the heat duty required to alter the temperature of the fluid. Heating the LP solution causes the carbamate to decompose into ![][image7] and ![][image6] vapors. This phase change and chemical dissociation absorbs vast quantities of latent heat, creating an immense "thermal sink." The temperature of the fluid will rise far slower than predicted by the frozen ![][image1] alone24.  
Conversely, in the LP carbamate condenser, the cooling of the mixed gases into the liquid phase drives carbamate formation. This phase change releases massive exothermic heat that makes the *effective* specific heat of the condensing fluid appear extraordinarily high. The condenser must therefore be sized not just to cool the fluid, but to remove the intense heat of chemical reaction. The equilibrium constants dictating this behavior (![][image56] for carbamate formation and ![][image57] for decomposition) are heavily temperature-dependent. In rigorous simulation models, the natural log of the equilibrium constant ![][image58] is expressed as a function of temperature parameters (![][image59]), ensuring the apparent heat capacity perfectly tracks the extent of reaction2.

## **Process Technology Variants and LP Condenser Operations**

In industrial practice, urea technology licensors dictate the operation of their LP sections based on highly specific stoichiometric constraints. The exact composition of the LP carbamate solution is dictated by the ![][image4] (N/C) ratio and the ![][image5] (H/C) ratio originating from the HP synthesis loop and the MP section6. Because the composition dictates the baseline heat capacity, different technologies possess inherently different thermal profiles.

### **Stamicarbon ![][image6]\-Stripping Process**

The Stamicarbon process is characterized by an overall lower N/C ratio (approximately 2.95 in the reactor)27. Because stripping is achieved using ![][image6] gas as the stripping agent, the amount of free, unreacted ammonia traversing the downstream sections is minimized. Consequently, the LP carbamate solution is relatively lean in free ammonia. Because liquid ammonia has an exceptionally high specific heat, the Stamicarbon LP carbamate solution generally exhibits a lower baseline frozen specific heat compared to high-ammonia variants. This allows for tighter thermal control but requires careful monitoring of the H/C ratio to prevent the solution from crossing the solid carbamate solubility threshold and precipitating.

### **Snamprogetti / Saipem ![][image7]\-Stripping Process**

The Snamprogetti process operates with a highly elevated N/C ratio (typically 3.2 to 3.6)6. The stripping action relies heavily on the self-stripping mechanism of the excess ammonia combined with thermal heat. Therefore, the LP carbamate solution arriving at the LP condenser contains a vast excess of free liquid ammonia. As established previously, liquid ammonia exhibits a ![][image1] exceeding 5.0 to 6.0 kJ/(kg·K) at these elevated temperatures11. The baseline frozen specific heat of a Snamprogetti LP carbamate solution will systematically be higher than that of a Stamicarbon LP solution, requiring larger sensible heat duties but providing a robust buffer against localized temperature spikes during exothermic condensation.

### **Toyo ACES21 Process**

The advanced Cost and Energy Saving (ACES) process pioneered by Toyo Engineering employs an intermediate N/C ratio9. Toyo heavily utilizes advanced Vertical Submerged Carbamate Condensers (VSCC) to maximize heat recovery. In the VSCC, the high apparent specific heat of the carbamate formation is brilliantly leveraged; the intense exothermic heat of reaction is transferred directly through the condenser tubes to generate low-pressure steam or preheat boiler feedwater, massively improving the overall energy efficiency of the plant30.

### **Generalized Engineering Estimation**

For preliminary sizing or rapid auditing, a generalized engineering approximation for the pseudo-frozen specific heat (![][image60]) of a standard LP carbamate solution (e.g., comprising 40 wt% carbamate, 25 wt% free ammonia, and 35 wt% water) at 80 °C (353.15 K) can be estimated via simple mass-fraction (![][image61]) weighting:  
![][image62]  
Using the empirically derived values:  
![][image63]  
This bulk value of \~3.64 kJ/(kg·K) represents a highly reliable midpoint for heat exchanger shell-side sensible heat calculations, provided the rigorous reactive condensation enthalpy is handled separately in the overall overall heat transfer duty.

## **Thermal Integration, Corrosion, and Metallurgical Implications**

The complex interplay between the specific heat capacity of the carbamate solution and the exothermic heat of formation forms the core of LP thermal integration. In modern urea plants, the heat released in the LP carbamate condenser is never simply discarded to an open cooling water tower; instead, it is critically utilized to preheat liquid ammonia feed or to evaporate water in the downstream vacuum concentration sections, driving the urea melt to 99.6% purity for prilling or granulation4.  
However, the specific heat and specific volume characteristics of carbamate solutions pose severe constraints on the choice of metallurgy. Ammonium carbamate at LP conditions—where water concentration is high and temperatures approach 100 °C—is violently corrosive to standard carbon steels. It rapidly strips away protective iron oxides, leading to catastrophic thinning of the metal wall6. The formation and maintenance of the protective passive layer on stainless steels requires a delicate balance of continuous oxygen injection (passivation air) and precise temperature control.  
To withstand this aggressive environment, LP carbamate condensers, decomposers, and associated piping are constructed from specialized, highly alloyed austenitic and duplex stainless steels, most notably 2RE69 (UNS S31050) and DP28W.  
The thermal properties of these metals must be carefully matched against the fluid they contain. For example, DP28W exhibits a specific heat capacity of 519 J/(kg·K) at 100 °C, rising slightly to 547 J/(kg·K) at 150 °C31. Similarly, 2RE69 (724L) exhibits a specific heat of 480 J/(kg·K) at 100 °C33.  
The vast discrepancy between the low specific heat capacity of the metallic tube wall (![][image64] kJ/kg·K) and the exceptionally high apparent heat capacity of the reacting carbamate solution (![][image65] kJ/kg·K \+ latent heat of reaction) dictates the severity of the transient thermal stresses experienced by the equipment. During plant startups, shutdowns, or load changes, rapid shifts in the carbamate reaction equilibrium can release or absorb massive amounts of heat. Because the dense liquid can store and transfer this heat much faster than the metallic tubes can thermally equilibrate, severe thermal shocks can occur, potentially leading to stress corrosion cracking or mechanical fatigue at the tube-to-tubesheet welds6. This risk is extensively mitigated by the high mass-flow designs of vertical flooded condensers, which use the immense thermal inertia of the pooled carbamate solution to dampen sudden temperature excursions.

## **Conclusion**

The rigorous thermodynamic characterization of the specific heat capacity of ammonium carbamate solutions in the Low-Pressure recovery section of urea plants is a multi-layered engineering challenge. The thermal profile of this fluid cannot be accurately represented by a singular static value due to the profound, cross-linked impact of ionic speciation, non-ideal solvent interactions, and extreme temperature dependence.  
For pure or pseudo-component mathematical representations, the cubic polynomial ![][image66] provides an excellent empirical baseline for the hypothetical sub-cooled, non-reactive state18. However, accurate, industrial-scale plant simulation strictly requires the implementation of advanced activity coefficient models. Both the Extended UNIQUAC and e-NRTL frameworks resolve the highly non-ideal nature of the fluid by establishing the infinite dilution heat capacity of the carbamate anion at \-30.710 J/(mol·K), properly accounting for the severe electrostriction of the aqueous solvent shell surrounding the dense ions13.  
Ultimately, process engineers and plant designers must critically differentiate between the *frozen* heat capacity of the fluid—which typically rests between 3.2 and 3.8 kJ/(kg·K) depending on the excess ammonia ratio utilized by Stamicarbon or Snamprogetti technologies—and the *apparent* reactive heat capacity. The apparent specific heat is artificially and massively amplified by the latent heat of carbamate formation and decomposition occurring dynamically within the equipment24. Mastery of these thermodynamic profiles enables the optimization of LP carbamate condensers, ensures strict control over the plant's delicate water balance, and maintains the stringent thermal constraints required to protect highly alloyed metallurgy from corrosive degradation and thermal shock.

#### **Works cited**

> 11. PROCESS FOR RECOVERING EXCESS AMMONIA IN UREA SYNTHESIS BY \- UreaKnowHow, [https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/082\_1971%20Otsuka%20Mitsui%203573173%20recovery%20ammonia.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/082_1971%20Otsuka%20Mitsui%203573173%20recovery%20ammonia.pdf)

> 13. Thermodynamic Modeling of the NH3–CO2–H2O System with Electrolyte NRTL Model | Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research \- ACS Publications, [https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/ie201276m](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/ie201276m)  
> 14. Heat of absorption of CO2 in aqueous ammonia and ammonium carbonate/carbamate solutions | Request PDF \- ResearchGate, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/229339540\_Heat\_of\_absorption\_of\_CO2\_in\_aqueous\_ammonia\_and\_ammonium\_carbonatecarbamate\_solutions](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/229339540_Heat_of_absorption_of_CO2_in_aqueous_ammonia_and_ammonium_carbonatecarbamate_solutions)  
> 15. Urea Production Process Analysis | PDF \- Scribd, [https://www.scribd.com/document/692231713/Material-Energy-Balance-v-04](https://www.scribd.com/document/692231713/Material-Energy-Balance-v-04)  
> 16. (PDF) MANUFACTURE OF UREA BY SNAMPROGETTI PROCESS \- ResearchGate, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279525020\_MANUFACTURE\_OF\_UREA\_BY\_SNAMPROGETTI\_PROCESS](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279525020_MANUFACTURE_OF_UREA_BY_SNAMPROGETTI_PROCESS)  
> 17. Properties and Production Methods | PDF | Urea | Ammonia \- Scribd, [https://www.scribd.com/document/402934908/Urea-Kirk-Othmer-pdf](https://www.scribd.com/document/402934908/Urea-Kirk-Othmer-pdf)  
> 18. [https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/2022-Chauhan-Urea-Project-Report.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/2022-Chauhan-Urea-Project-Report.pdf)  
> 19. Comparison of Models for Calculation of the Thermodynamic Properties of NH3-CO2-H2O Mixture \- Purdue e-Pubs, [https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2640\&context=iracc](https://docs.lib.purdue.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2640&context=iracc)  
> 20. A low energy aqueous ammonia CO2 capture process, [https://backend.orbit.dtu.dk/ws/portalfiles/portal/105672122/A\_low\_energy\_aqueous\_ammonia.pdf](https://backend.orbit.dtu.dk/ws/portalfiles/portal/105672122/A_low_energy_aqueous_ammonia.pdf)  
> 21. Thermodynamic modeling of the NH3–CO2–H2O system with electrolyte NRTL model | Request PDF \- ResearchGate, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/263944280\_Thermodynamic\_modeling\_of\_the\_NH3-CO2-H2O\_system\_with\_electrolyte\_NRTL\_model](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/263944280_Thermodynamic_modeling_of_the_NH3-CO2-H2O_system_with_electrolyte_NRTL_model)  
> 22. Thermodynamic Modeling of the NH3–CO2–H2O System with Electrolyte NRTL Model, [http://a.xueshu.baidu.com/usercenter/paper/show?paperid=975dff106e43e8cd739c7abb73584e20](http://a.xueshu.baidu.com/usercenter/paper/show?paperid=975dff106e43e8cd739c7abb73584e20)

> 24. A Direct Synthetic Urea Process \- UreaKnowHow, [https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/532\_1930%20Krase%20Bureau%20of%20Chemistry%20and%20Soils%20A%20Direct%20Synthetic%20Urea%20Process.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/532_1930%20Krase%20Bureau%20of%20Chemistry%20and%20Soils%20A%20Direct%20Synthetic%20Urea%20Process.pdf)  
> 25. Comparative Study of Cocurrent and Countercurrent Modes of Operation for a Thermal Hydrolyzer in an Industrial Urea Wastewater Treatment Loop \- UreaKnowHow, [https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/2010-Rahimpour-Comparative-Study-of-Cocurrent-and-Countercurrent-Modes-of-Operation-for-a-Thermal-Hydrolyzer-in-an-Industrial-Urea-Wastewater-Treatment-Loop.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/2010-Rahimpour-Comparative-Study-of-Cocurrent-and-Countercurrent-Modes-of-Operation-for-a-Thermal-Hydrolyzer-in-an-Industrial-Urea-Wastewater-Treatment-Loop.pdf)  
> 26. (Fritz Ullmann, Matthias Bohnet) Ullmann S Encyclo | PDF \- Scribd, [https://de.scribd.com/document/369973106/Fritz-Ullmann-Matthias-Bohnet-Ullmann-s-Encyclo-BookSee-org](https://de.scribd.com/document/369973106/Fritz-Ullmann-Matthias-Bohnet-Ullmann-s-Encyclo-BookSee-org)

> 28. The Urea Synthesis Reactor, its theoretical model and its industrial applications Introduction Part \- UreaKnowHow, [https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/388\_2009%2005%20Shen%20SRIC%20UreaKnowHow.com%20Urea%20synthesis%20dynamic%20model%20Introduction.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/pdflib/388_2009%2005%20Shen%20SRIC%20UreaKnowHow.com%20Urea%20synthesis%20dynamic%20model%20Introduction.pdf)

Alleima® 3R60 Urea Grade \- UreaKnowHow, [https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/datasheet-alleima-3r60-urea-grade-en-v2023-03-30-11\_47-version-1-Copy.pdf](https://ureaknowhow.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/datasheet-alleima-3r60-urea-grade-en-v2023-03-30-11_47-version-1-Copy.pdf)
